verses.text: ‘My soul is weary of my life.’ Job permits himself to lament. Scripture leaves space for the bitter prayer — the worn-out soul who still speaks to God. The Hebrew qatsar nafshi (literally ‘my soul is short’) evokes someone whose breath is running out. Faith here is not a smile; it is the refusal to stop speaking.

Job asks God not to condemn him without explanation. The Hebrew rib — contend, dispute — names what Job is asking for: a hearing. He wants God’s case against him laid out. In a culture where one might expect bowed silence, Job’s honest demand for understanding is itself a kind of faith. He talks to God because he still believes God listens.

Three rapid-fire questions to God: Is it good to oppress? To despise your own handiwork? To bless the schemes of the wicked? Job’s theology is being tested in real time. He does not stop believing; he stops pretending. The book of Job leaves these questions on the page without scrubbing them, signalling that anguished honesty is allowed in the presence of God.

‘Hast thou eyes of flesh?’ Job asks whether God sees like a human does — limited, deceived by appearances. The question is poignant: Job suspects God might be misreading him. In the end, God’s answer (Job 38-41) will reveal sight so vast it dwarfs Job’s. But here, in the dark, the question is honest.

Are God’s days like human days, that He has to rush His judgment? Job presses the asymmetry: surely an eternal God need not pursue him so quickly. The verse is a thought experiment in lament — Job using God’s own attributes as the basis for his complaint. Even his protest is theologically informed.

‘That thou enquirest after mine iniquity.’ Job feels that God is hunting him for sin. The Hebrew baqash — to seek out diligently — paints God as an investigator. Job has not yet heard the heavenly courtroom scene of Job 1-2; from his perspective, this suffering looks like prosecution. The reader knows differently. So often the story we are inside is not the only story.

‘Thou knowest that I am not wicked.’ Job appeals to God’s own knowledge against the verdict his friends keep declaring. The Hebrew yada — to know intimately — means more than awareness; it is the deep knowing of a relationship. Job’s last comfort is that the God who searches him also truly knows him. Even in lament, he trusts that knowing.

10:8-11 A succession of images describes God’s role in creating Job. • You formed me with your hands . . . made me from dust: This was how a potter made a vessel (Isa 45:9; Jer 18:1-12). Although the potter has incontestable sovereignty over the clay (Isa 45:9; Jer 18:5-12; Rom 9:20-25), Job was questioning God. • back to dust: This common motif in Job represents the temporary nature of life (Job 4:19; 7:9-10, 21; 14:1-2, 10; see Gen 3:19). • You guided my conception and formed me in the womb: The Hebrew terminology (literally You poured me out like milk and curdled me like cheese) evokes graphic images of a newborn baby still covered with the creamy substance called vernix.

10:12 And these things thou hast hid in thine heart,.... Meaning, either the mercies and favours he had indulged him with; these he seemed to conceal and suppress the memory of, as if they had never been, by a different conduct and behaviour; or rather, these he had laid up in his mind and memory, and had full knowledge and remembrance of; though he dealt with him in the manner he did, he could not forget his former favours to him, which, when compared with his present dealings, were very unlike: or, it may be best to understand these things of his afflictions and troubles, which, notwithstanding his being the work of his hand so curiously formed, and notwithstanding all his temporal and spiritual mercies, he had in his heart purposed,…

10:13 If I sin, then thou markest me,.... Or "observest me" (t); that is, he took notice of his sins, strictly inquired into them and all the circumstances of them, watched the motions and progress of them, and carefully laid them up, in order to bring them out against him another day, and afflict or punish him for them; or he set a watch about him, "kept him in" (u), and enclosed him on every side with affliction, as if he was in a watch or prison, as Gersom;

10:14 If I be wicked, woe is me,.... In this world, and to all eternity; afflictions will abide me here, and everlasting wrath hereafter: these are the woes that belong to a wicked man; that is, a profane and abandoned sinner, that lives in sin, and gives up himself to all manner of wickedness; the Targum is,"destruction to me from the great judgment;''utter ruin is my portion, as it is of all wicked and unrighteous persons, Isa 3:11, and if I be righteous, yet will I not lift up my head;

10:15 For it increaseth,.... That is, the affliction increaseth; which is a reason why pity should be shown him, seeing his troubles instead of abating were growing upon him; he had as much, or more, than he could well bear, and yet more was added to it; so that he was an object of compassion: or, "it lifteth itself up" (c); these proud waves of affliction rise, swell, and lift themselves on high, and threaten to overwhelm and utterly destroy; some render it as a "wish, oh, that it increased" (d);
10:16 increaseth—rather, "(if) I lift up (my head) Thou wouldest hunt me," &c. [UMBREIT]. and again—as if a lion should not kill his prey at once, but come back and torture it again.

10:17 witnesses—His accumulated trials were like a succession of witnesses brought up in proof of his guilt, to wear out the accused. changes and war—rather, "(thou settest in array) against me host after host" (literally, "changes and a host," that is, a succession of hosts); namely, his afflictions, and then reproach upon reproach from his friends.

10:18 I should have been as though I had not been,.... For though it cannot be said absolutely of such an one, an abortive or untimely birth, that it is a nonentity, or never existed; yet comparatively it is as if it never had a being; it being seen by none or very few, it having had no name, nor any conversation among men; but at once buried, and buried in forgetfulness, as if no such one had ever been; see Ecc 6:3.

10:19 Are not my days few?.... They are so, the days of every man are but few; see Job 14:1; the remainder of Job's days were but few; considering the course of nature, and especially the sore afflictions he had on him, it could not be thought his days on earth were many; in all likelihood, according to human probability, he had but a few days to live: or "are not my days a small little thing" (g)? it is as an hand's breadth, as nothing before God, Ps 39:5, cease then;
10:20-22 Earlier, Job had painted a fairly pleasant picture of Sheol (3:13-19); now he describes it as a land of darkness and utter gloom (see also 7:9-10; 14:7-22).
10:20-22 Earlier, Job had painted a fairly pleasant picture of Sheol (3:13-19); now he describes it as a land of darkness and utter gloom (see also 7:9-10; 14:7-22).
